title: Multiple Modular Values for SL2(Z)
speaker: Francis Brown
tags: Workshop on Fundamental Groups and Periods

proof_stages.label: Mixed Tate prototype from P^1 minus three points
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls how unipotent completions of fundamental groupoids of P^1\{0,1,infinity} acquire motivic structures via Beilinson relative homology, tangential basepoints, and the Deligne-Goncharov mixed Tate category, with periods given by regularized iterated integrals and multiple zeta values.

proof_stages.label: Motivation for replacing punctured lines by modular curves
proof_stages.summary: The speaker argues that the punctured-line mixed Tate story is too large and too externally controlled, and proposes replacing it by quotients of the upper half-plane, relative completion, multiple modular values, and a conjectural category of mixed modular motives.

proof_stages.label: SL2Z modular forms as differential forms with cusp residues
proof_stages.summary: For SL2(Z), modular forms with coefficients in polynomial representations V_n are converted into invariant differential forms with explicit residues at the cusp, recovering classical Eichler-Shimura-Manin and Zagier length-one periods.

proof_stages.label: Tangential-cusp regularization of iterated modular integrals
proof_stages.summary: Regularized iterated integrals of arbitrary modular forms to the tangential cusp are defined by subtracting Eisenstein residues with an explicit operator, producing exponentially convergent polynomial-valued periods.

proof_stages.label: Canonical cocycle and definition of multiple modular values
proof_stages.summary: The generating series of regularized iterated integrals yields a canonical SL2(Z) one-cocycle, determined by its values on S and T, with the explicit T-part separated so that multiple modular values are the coefficients of the S-part.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic content of double Eisenstein integrals
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that iterated integrals of two Eisenstein series already contain depth-two-to-four multiple zeta values and noncritical L-values of modular forms, indicating that the modular construction captures rich mixed Tate and modular extensions.

proof_stages.label: Automorphism constraints from relative completion and cusp monodromy
proof_stages.summary: Assuming a mixed modular motivic Galois group, its unipotent radical should act on the relative completion while respecting the SL2 map and fixing cusp monodromy, reducing possible actions to automorphisms satisfying a fixed-point equation involving kappa.

proof_stages.label: Extraction of Eisenstein and cuspidal Galois-type generators
proof_stages.summary: Using the free Lie algebra description of the relative completion and the cusp constraint, the speaker identifies expected Eisenstein/mixed Tate and cuspidal generators and interprets their components as zeta values, Manin relations, Hain's Eisenstein quotient action, and prescriptions for iterated extensions.

examples.name: The n=2 relative-homology triangle
examples.note: The case n=2 is drawn as a triangle to make visible why the simplex attached to a path has boundary in the divisor Y_n.
examples.engagement_reason: He runs the relative-homology construction using the simplex and divisors, specializing visually to n=2 as a triangle whose boundary lies in the relative divisor.

examples.name: Cyclotomic punctures and Goncharov's failure cases
examples.note: The family P^1 minus 0, infinity, and roots of unity, especially the failure for primes n >= 11, is used as a motivating counterexample pushing the talk toward modular geometry.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites the cyclotomic puncture cases and Goncharov’s prime-failure result as known facts without computing or checking them.

examples.name: Belyi-theorem richness: H^1 of algebraic curves
